retrieved_docs.title: Dopamine Agonists -- Administration -- Hypertensive Emergency
retrieved_docs.content: Fenoldopam is administered by continuous intravenous infusion using a calibrated infusion pump. The starting dose in adults is 0.1 to 0.3 microgram/kg/min. This low initial dose is recommended to prevent reflex tachycardia. Dose is increased by 0.05 to 0.1 microgram/kg/min every 15 to 20 minutes. The maximum dose is 1.6 microgram/kg/min. Its use should be limited to not more than 48 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Inotropes and Vasopressors -- Mechanism of Action -- Catecholamines
retrieved_docs.content: Dopamine is a precursor of norepinephrine and epinephrine, which acts in a dose-dependent fashion on dopaminergic receptors and α and β receptors. At low doses, dopaminergic receptors activate renal artery vasodilation. At doses 5 to 15 μg/kg/min, α and β-adrenergic activation increase renal blood flow, HR, contractility, and CO. At higher doses greater than 15 μg/kg/min, the main effects are on α stimulation. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Dopamine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Dopamine (DA) is a peripheral vasostimulant used to treat low blood pressure, low heart rate, and cardiac arrest, especially in acute neonatal cases via a continuous intravenous drip. [1] Low infusion rates (0.5 to 2 micrograms/kg per minute) act on the visceral vasculature to produce vasodilation, including the kidneys, resulting in increased urinary flow. Intermediate infusion rates (from 2 to 10 micrograms/kg/min) stimulate myocardial contractility and increase electrical conductivity in the heart leading to increased cardiac output.  Higher doses cause vasoconstriction and increased blood pressure via the adrenergic receptors alpha-1, beta-1, and beta-2, potentially leading to poor peripheral circulation. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Dopamine Agonists -- Indications -- Dopamine Agonists to Treat Hypertensive Emergency
retrieved_docs.content: Fenoldopam is a selective D1 receptor agonist that causes vasodilation of renal, splanchnic, and coronary arteries as well as blood vessels in the skeletal muscles. After intravenous injection, the onset of drug action is rapid (10 minutes), and the dose-response relationship is linear. It also has a short elimination half-life. [10] Therefore, fenoldopam is recommended for the parenteral treatment of hypertensive emergencies in a setting of acute kidney injury or cerebrovascular accident. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Dopamine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dopamine is a peripheral vasostimulant used to treat low blood pressure, low heart rate, and cardiac arrest. Low infusion rates (0.5 to 2 micrograms/kg per minute) act on the visceral vasculature to produce vasodilation, including the kidneys, resulting in increased urinary flow. Intermediate infusion rates (from 2 to 10 micrograms/kg/min) stimulate myocardial contractility and increase electrical conductivity in the heart leading to increased cardiac output. Higher doses cause vasoconstriction and increased blood pressure via the adrenergic receptors alpha-1, beta-1, and beta-2, potentially leading to poor peripheral circulation. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, pharmacology, monitoring, and relevant interactions of doxorubicin, pertinent for members of the interprofessional team in the treatment of patients with indicated conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Catecholamines -- Mechanism -- Dopamine
retrieved_docs.content: Although dopamine primarily acts on dopamine receptors, it can also interact with adrenergic receptors, especially in peripheral tissues. At moderate concentrations, dopamine can stimulate β1-adrenergic receptors, increasing heart rate and contractility. At high concentrations, dopamine may also activate α1-adrenergic receptors, leading to vasoconstriction and increased blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Inotropes and Vasopressors -- Mechanism of Action -- Catecholamines
retrieved_docs.content: Phenylephrine is a pure α1 agonist, inducing peripheral arterial vasoconstriction. Reflex bradycardia may occur due to selective vasoconstriction and elevation of blood pressure. Blood pressure, MAP, and SVR are increased. [5] Norepinephrine exhibits mixed α1 and β activity (β1 more than β2), with slightly more α1 activity than β activity. This leads to a more significant increase in blood pressure than increased HR. Blood pressure, MAP, SVR, and CO are increased with norepinephrine. [6] Epinephrine has essentially comparable activity on α1 and β receptors. Epinephrine increases systemic vascular resistance, heart rate, cardiac output, and blood pressure. [6] [1] Isoproterenol is an isopropylamine analog of epinephrine used in bradyarrhythmias (eg, torsades de pointes) and Brugada syndrome. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Dopamine Agonists -- Mechanism of Action -- Dopamine Agonists to Treat Hypertensive Emergency
retrieved_docs.content: The D1 receptors are present on the smooth muscle of the renal, mesenteric, and coronary arteries and peripheral blood vessels in the skeletal muscle. Dopamine action on these receptors produces decreased blood pressure by reducing peripheral vascular resistance due to vasodilation. Dopamine agonists used to treat hypertensive emergencies do not show an affinity for D2 receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Catecholamines -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Epinephrine is used in anaphylactic shock to stimulate both α- and β-receptors, leading to vasoconstriction, bronchodilation, and increased heart rate and cardiac output. Norepinephrine is a first-line vasopressor in septic shock, primarily stimulating α1-receptors to increase vascular tone and blood pressure while having less impact on heart rate than other agents. Dopamine causes vasodilation at low doses but stimulates β1- and α1-adrenergic receptors at higher doses, increasing cardiac output and blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Encephalopathy -- Treatment Planning -- Parenteral Antihypertensives
retrieved_docs.content: Fenoldopam is a selective dopamine-1 agonist.  This drug decreases peripheral vascular resistance and increases renal blood flow. The initial infusion dose is 0.1 mcg/kg per minute, with the dose titrated at 15-minute intervals, depending on patient response. A maximum infusion rate of 1.6 mcg/kg per minute has been reported in clinical trials. Fenoldopam is especially beneficial in patients with kidney disease. However, the medication increases intraocular pressure and should be used cautiously in patients with glaucoma. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Inotropes and Vasopressors -- Mechanism of Action -- Catecholamines
retrieved_docs.content: Dobutamine increases CO mostly through its effects on β and α stimulation. Dobutamine has an affinity for β1 greater than β2 greater than α. Dobutamine increases contractility and CO with minimal effects on BP. [6] [1] Dobutamine is also used in cardiac stress testing. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Dopamine -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: According to " Sax's Dangerous Properties of Industrial Materials " (by Richard J. Lewis, 2004 - DOI: 10.1002/0471701343), studies in rodents showed an LD50 (a lethal dose in 50% of subjects) between 59 to 163 mg/kg. In humans, dopamine can lead to peripheral vasoconstriction resulting in gangrenous extremities and cardiac arrhythmias. [44] [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Inotropes and Vasopressors -- Mechanism of Action -- Catecholamines
retrieved_docs.content: The most common catecholamine-active medications are phenylephrine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine. Other agents in this class include isoproterenol, dobutamine, and dopamine. Each of these 3 medications has varying activity on the α and β receptors. Alpha receptors are peripheral vasoconstrictors that increase SVR. Beta-1 receptors have mostly positive chronotropic (heart rate) and inotropic (contractility) effects on the heart. Beta-2 receptors act as vasodilators in many organ systems. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Inotropes and Vasopressors -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Patients currently taking an MAOI will have a decreased metabolism of adrenergic vasopressors and will require lower doses to avoid toxicity. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Dopamine -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with conditions of the heart or circulatory system, the intravenous administration of dopamine is contraindicated. These conditions may include ventricular arrhythmias and tachycardia, blood vessel blockage, low blood-oxygen content, decreased blood volume, acidosis, and adrenal gland dysfunctions resulting in high blood pressure such as pheochromocytoma. For patients recently treated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors, DA is initially used in fractional doses (one-tenth usual dose) and closely monitored for further effects. Medications to treat hypertension, such as beta and alpha-adrenergic inhibitors, antagonize the therapeutic effects of DA. Haloperidol also blocks the systemic effects of DA. The anticonvulsant phenytoin has been reported to cause hypotension and decreased heart rate when used with DA. On the other hand, tricyclic antidepressants increase the DA response, similar to anesthetic agents such as cyclopropane and halogenated. In combination with oxytocin, the use of DA can lead to chronic hypertension and may also cause cerebrovascular accidents. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Vasoplegic Syndrome and Noncatecholamine Therapies -- Treatment / Management -- Vasopressin
retrieved_docs.content: When high doses of catecholamines are ineffective for maintaining blood pressure or result in adverse effects, vasopressin may be utilized as an additional treatment option. Vasopressin (at doses up to 0.03 µmol/min) is used as a second-line or extra treatment for catecholamine-resistant vasodilatory shock. [19] By acting on the vasopressin receptor 1, it reinstates vascular tone. Vasopressin directly deactivates the K-ATP channel, inhibiting the increase in cGMP caused by NO, reducing NO synthesis, and minimizing the effects of membrane hyperpolarization, myosin dephosphorylation, and NO accumulation. [20] These actions make vasopressin a viable alternative when high doses of catecholamines are ineffective or unsafe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1386
retrieved_docs.content: Amphetamine is classified as a vasoconstrictor, indicating that it belongs to the pharmacological group of agents that cause narrowing of blood vessels and influence vascular tone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1082
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic and dopaminergic cardiac stimulants constitute a pharmacologic class of agents that enhance myocardial contractility and cardiac output through combined stimulation of adrenergic and dopaminergic receptors. Representative members of this class include cafedrine, dobutamin, fenoldopam, metaraminol, and theodrenaline. Cafedrine exerts its cardiac effects by synergistically activating α‑adrenergic and β‑adrenergic pathways, leading to increased heart rate and contractile force. Dobutamin primarily stimulates β1‑adrenergic receptors with modest β2 and α1 activity, providing inotropic support while minimizing tachycardia. Fenoldopam is a selective dopamine‑1 receptor agonist that produces vasodilation and modest positive inotropic effects, useful in managing acute hypertension and low‑output states. Metaraminol acts mainly as an α1‑adrenergic agonist with indirect β‑adrenergic activity, raising systemic vascular resistance and supporting blood pressure in shock. Theodrenaline combines α‑adrenergic vasoconstriction with β‑adrenergic cardiac stimulation, offering balanced hemodynamic support. Collectively, these drugs are employed in critical care and perioperative settings to stabilize hemodynamics, improve tissue perfusion, and treat conditions such as heart failure, cardiogenic shock, and severe hypotension. Understanding the shared mechanisms and distinct receptor profiles of cafedrine, dobutamin, fenoldopam, metaraminol, and theodrenaline aids clinicians in selecting the most appropriate agent for individualized patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1397
retrieved_docs.content: Dipyridamol is classified as a vasodilator, indicating its therapeutic role in widening blood vessels to improve blood flow. Pindolol is also identified as a vasodilator, reflecting its ability to contribute to vascular relaxation alongside its beta‑blocking properties. In contrast, dihydroergokryptin is not considered a vasodilator, distinguishing it from the other two agents and highlighting that its pharmacologic actions do not primarily involve vascular dilation. Recognizing which agents act as vasodilators and which do not is important for selecting appropriate medications in conditions requiring modulation of vascular tone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770
retrieved_docs.content: Dopamine is classified as a sympathomimetic, meaning it mimics the actions of the sympathetic nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 824
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic antagonists are classified as a type of neurotransmitter antagonist, reflecting their role in blocking the actions of catecholamine neurotransmitters at adrenergic receptors. Within this pharmacologic group are several specific agents, including dihydroergocristine, its mesylate salt (dihydroergocristine mesylate), fentolamine, and oxidopamine. These compounds share the common mechanism of inhibiting adrenergic signaling, which can be utilized therapeutically to modulate sympathetic nervous system activity in various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1855
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), abdominal pain (ألم بطني), and increased blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم). The vasoconstrictive and pro‑thrombotic effects of cocaine can precipitate acute myocardial ischemia, manifesting as unstable angina, while its sympathomimetic activity commonly raises systemic arterial pressure. Additionally, cocaine‑induced visceral vasospasm or ischemia may present as abdominal pain. Recognizing these interrelated clinical manifestations is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of patients who use cocaine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2584
retrieved_docs.content: Sildenafil and tadalafil are phosphodiesterase‑5 inhibitor medications that are contraindicated for use with nitroglycerin, a nitrate vasodilator; concurrent administration of either sildenafil or tadalafil with nitroglycerin can lead to severe hypotension and should be avoided in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 61
retrieved_docs.content: families. Collectively, these relationships map how phosphodiesterase inhibition, cardiac stimulant activity, vasodilatory effects, and renin‑angiotensin system modulation are interconnected yet pharmacologically distinct, providing a nuanced framework for therapeutic decision‑making, contraindication management, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1490
retrieved_docs.content: Dopamine and the combination of salmeterol and fluticasone are both classified as sympathomimetic agents. As sympathomimetics, they mimic the actions of the sympathetic nervous system, producing effects such as increased heart rate, vasoconstriction, and bronchodilation. Dopamine exerts its therapeutic effects by stimulating dopaminergic and adrenergic receptors, making it useful in the management of conditions like heart failure, cardiogenic shock, and certain types of hypotension. The combination of salmeterol, a long‑acting β2‑adrenergic agonist, and fluticasone, an inhaled corticosteroid, is employed primarily in the treatment of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; salmeterol’s β2‑agonist activity provides bronchodilation through sympathomimetic mechanisms, while fluticasone reduces airway inflammation. Recognizing both dopamine and the salmeterol‑fluticasone combination as sympathomimetics highlights their shared pharmacologic class despite their different clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1365
retrieved_docs.content: Terazosin is an α₁‑adrenergic antagonist that produces arterial vasodilation, a physiologic effect that reduces peripheral vascular resistance and lowers systemic blood pressure. Because of this vasodilatory action, terazosin is contraindicated in patients with ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), as further reduction in vascular tone could precipitate symptomatic hypotension. Although one source incorrectly lists terazosin as having “Endocrine drugs” as a parent category, terazosin is more appropriately classified as a cardiovascular medication rather than an endocrine drug. Recognizing terazosin’s arterial vasodilation effect and its contraindication in hypotensive states is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Papaverin produces vasodilation as its physiologic effect, meaning the drug induces relaxation of vascular smooth muscle and widening of blood vessels, underscoring its role as a vasodilator in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2743
retrieved_docs.content: Amfetamin is classified as a vasoconstrictor, and angiotensin amide is also identified as a vasoconstrictor. Both agents belong to the group of substances that cause narrowing of blood vessels, thereby influencing vascular tone and blood pressure regulation. Understanding that these drugs share vasoconstrictive properties is important for anticipating their hemodynamic effects and for managing patients who may be exposed to one or both agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1326
retrieved_docs.content: Methoxyfluran and celiprolol are pharmacologic agents that both produce vasodilation as a physiologic effect. Vasodilation [PE] refers to the widening of blood vessels, which decreases vascular resistance and can enhance tissue perfusion or lower blood pressure. Recognizing that methoxyfluran and celiprolol each induce vasodilation helps clinicians understand their therapeutic potential in conditions where reduced vascular tone or improved blood flow is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] exert their pharmacologic effect by antagonizing beta‑1 adrenergic receptors, thereby reducing sympathetic stimulation of the heart and other tissues. Because this mechanism opposes the cholinergic activity of methacholin, the use of Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] is contraindicated in patients receiving methacholin, as the conflicting actions can lead to adverse physiological effects. Clinicians should therefore avoid combining these agents to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic and dopaminergic cardiac stimulants constitute a pharmacologic class that includes arbutamine, cafedrine, dobutamin, fenoldopam, metaraminol, midodrin, theodrenaline and oktopamin. All of these agents share the property of stimulating cardiac activity through combined adrenergic and dopaminergic receptor mechanisms, which can enhance myocardial contractility and support blood pressure in clinical situations such as acute heart failure, cardiogenic shock, or severe hypotension. By acting on both α‑ and β‑adrenergic receptors as well as dopaminergic receptors, the drugs in this class provide a balanced inotropic and vasopressor effect, making them useful tools for clinicians managing patients with compromised cardiac output or vascular tone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1420
retrieved_docs.content: Vasodilation [PE] is identified as the physiologic effect produced by several pharmacologic agents, including papaverin, methoxyfluran, and celiprolol. Papaverin exerts its action by inducing vasodilation, while both methoxyfluran and celiprolol are described as having vasodilation as a primary physiologic effect. This shared mechanism of widening blood vessels underlies their therapeutic use in conditions where reduction of vascular resistance and improvement of tissue perfusion are desired.
